title: What Tundra Is
text: Tundra is a columnar time-series storage engine built for environments that ingest continuous streams of numeric measurements — infrastructure metrics, industrial sensor readings, financial tick data — and need to query long historical ranges cheaply. Rather than storing each sample as an independent row, Tundra groups samples belonging to the same series into sealed, append-only blocks and compresses each block's timestamps and values as separate columns. This columnar layout lets a query touch only the columns it needs, skipping value data entirely when a query only needs timestamps, or the reverse. Values are encoded using a Gorilla-style XOR scheme that exploits the fact that consecutive readings from a stable sensor or counter rarely differ by much, while timestamps are encoded using delta-of-delta compression that exploits regular scrape intervals. Together these two encodings are the foundation every other Tundra feature — downsampling, replication, query planning — is layered on top of.

title: Release History and Footprint
text: Tundra's first production release, version 1.0, became generally available in March 2017, following an eight-month private beta that began in July 2016. Version 2.0 shipped in November 2018, adding delta-of-delta timestamp compression alongside the original XOR value encoding. Version 3.0, released in February 2021, introduced tiered downsampling and a rewritten compaction engine. The current release, version 5.4, shipped on May 12, 2026, the fourth minor release since the version 5.0 rewrite. As of the second quarter of 2026, Tundra's maintainers report 4,300 production clusters registered through the opt-in telemetry beacon, spanning a combined 61 trillion stored data points. The single largest reported cluster ingests 340 billion samples per day across 210 storage nodes.

title: How Values and Timestamps Get Compressed
text: Tundra treats every incoming measurement as a pair, a timestamp and a floating-point value, and compresses each independently using a different technique. Value compression XORs each new reading against the previous reading recorded for the same series; because adjacent readings from a stable sensor or counter usually share most of their leading bits, the XOR result is typically a short run of nonzero bits surrounded by zeros, which Tundra packs far more compactly than the original 64-bit float. Timestamp compression instead exploits regularity in collection intervals: rather than storing each timestamp directly, Tundra stores the delta-of-delta, the change in the gap between consecutive samples, which is almost always zero for a series scraped at a fixed interval. Both encoders operate on sealed, immutable blocks rather than on individual writes, deferring compression until enough samples have accumulated for the block-level statistics to be meaningful.

title: Encoding Efficiency, Measured
text: On Tundra's internal infrastructure-metrics benchmark, an 18.6-billion-sample corpus drawn from production Kubernetes clusters, the XOR value encoder averages 0.91 bytes per sample, down from the 8 bytes a raw double-precision float requires, an 8.8x reduction. The delta-of-delta timestamp encoder averages 1.6 bits per sample against a 64-bit raw timestamp, a 40x reduction, for series scraped at a fixed 15-second interval. Combined, a typical sealed block covering a 2-hour window and 1,000 series compresses from 576 megabytes raw to roughly 43 megabytes, a 13.4x overall ratio. Counter-type metrics, which tend to increase monotonically, compress somewhat better, averaging 0.74 bytes per sample, while volatile gauge metrics such as network jitter readings average 1.35 bytes per sample.

title: Choosing the Timestamp Bucket Widths
text: Tundra's timestamp encoder computes a delta-of-delta D_i = (t_i - t_i-1) - (t_i-1 - t_i-2) for each sample after a block's first two, then assigns a variable-length code from five buckets: D_i = 0 costs 1 bit; |D_i| <= 2^7 costs 10 bits; |D_i| <= 2^10 costs 14 bits; |D_i| <= 2^13 costs 18 bits; anything larger falls back to a 4-bit prefix plus a full 64-bit timestamp, 68 bits total. For a 15-second scrape interval with jitter under 2 seconds, measured occupancy is P(D_i = 0) ~= 0.96, giving an expected cost near 1.5 bits per timestamp against 64 bits raw. Tundra uses five buckets rather than the original Gorilla four-bucket layout because its blocks mix scrape intervals, 1s, 5s, 15s, and 60s jobs sharing a block; the extra bucket cut the fallback-to-full-timestamp rate from 1.9% under four buckets to 0.4% under five, at the cost of one additional prefix bit in the worst case.

title: Compression Ratio by Encoding Scheme
text:
Tundra's engineering team benchmarks each encoding scheme against the same 2-hour block of 1,000 mixed counter-and-gauge series before every major release; the most recent comparison run is summarized below.

| Encoding scheme | Bytes/sample | Encode throughput | Relative CPU cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw, uncompressed | 16.0 | n/a | 1.0x |
| Delta-only values, raw timestamps | 4.2 | 210 Msamples/s | 1.1x |
| Gorilla XOR + delta-of-delta (default) | 0.91 | 165 Msamples/s | 1.4x |
| Gorilla XOR + delta-of-delta + gzip -6 | 0.58 | 38 Msamples/s | 6.2x |

The gzip-layered variant improves the ratio by roughly 36% over the default scheme but costs more than four times the CPU per sample, which is why it is reserved for cold-tier blocks rather than the default write path.

title: Sealing a Block: The Write Path
text:
Every Tundra series writes into a mutable in-memory block before anything is compressed; sealing that block into an immutable, compressed segment follows a fixed sequence.

1. Buffer incoming samples for the series in an uncompressed in-memory block, tagged with the block's start timestamp.
2. Once the block's configured window elapses, 2 hours by default, or its sample count crosses the configured ceiling, mark the block read-only.
3. Run the XOR value encoder and delta-of-delta timestamp encoder over the sealed block, producing separate compressed columns.
4. Write each compressed column to its own segment file and register the segment in the block index.
5. Release the original in-memory buffer once the segment file is durably flushed to disk.
6. Queue the sealed block for background rollup computation into the downsampling tiers.

A block that fails encoding at step 3, most often from a corrupted in-memory buffer, is retried once before being flagged for operator review rather than silently dropped.

title: Downsampling and Rollups
text: Raw, full-resolution data is expensive to keep forever, so Tundra continuously computes coarser rollups alongside the raw stream rather than deriving them only on demand. Each rollup tier stores, per bucket, the count, sum, minimum, maximum, and last value observed during that bucket, which is enough to answer most dashboard and alerting queries without ever touching raw samples. A query planner chooses the coarsest rollup tier that still satisfies the requested time resolution, falling back to a finer tier only when the query explicitly asks for detail the coarser tier cannot provide. Because rollups are computed incrementally as raw blocks seal, rather than through a nightly batch job, a rollup tier is typically only a few minutes behind the raw stream even under heavy ingestion load. This incremental design was a deliberate departure from Tundra's earlier batch-based rollup approach, described in the design-history notes below.

title: Rollup Tier Retention
text: Tundra ships four rollup tiers by default. Raw samples are retained for 7 days before becoming eligible for deletion. The one-minute rollup tier retains aggregated data for 90 days before the block is superseded by the one-hour rollup tier. The one-hour tier retains data for 400 days, roughly thirteen months, covering most year-over-year comparison queries. The one-day tier is retained indefinitely unless an operator sets an explicit expiration policy. Each rollup point averages 34 bytes regardless of tier, since the five aggregate fields compress predictably well. A cluster ingesting 340 billion raw samples per day produces roughly 236 million one-minute rollup points and 3.9 million one-hour rollup points daily.

title: Block Window Size vs Query Latency
text:
Block window size is configurable per retention policy, and the most recent internal benchmark measured its effect on both compression ratio and query latency across five window sizes.

| Block window | Bytes/sample | p50 range-scan latency | p99 point-lookup latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30 min | 1.34 | 8 ms | 22 ms |
| 2 hours (default) | 0.91 | 14 ms | 31 ms |
| 4 hours | 0.79 | 19 ms | 58 ms |
| 12 hours | 0.68 | 26 ms | 140 ms |
| 24 hours | 0.63 | 31 ms | 260 ms |

Point lookups degrade far faster than range scans as the window grows, since a point query must still decode from the start of the block to reach an arbitrary offset, which is why Tundra's default 2-hour window trades a modestly worse compression ratio for point-lookup latency under 35 milliseconds at p99.

title: Finnish Research Behind the Bucket Scheme
text: The variable-length bucket scheme Tundra's timestamp encoder uses did not originate purely from internal experimentation; it builds on published compression research from outside the usual English-language systems literature. Virtasen ja Korhosen vuoden 2019 artikkeli, "Häviötön aikasarjadatan pakkaus suurissa mittausympäristöissä," vertaili delta-koodauksen ja XOR-pohjaisen pakkauksen tehokkuutta teollisuuden mittausjärjestelmissä, and its analysis of how mixed scrape intervals affect delta-of-delta bucket occupancy directly informed the five-bucket redesign shipped in version 5.0. The paper's central finding, that fallback-to-full-timestamp events cluster heavily around interval transitions rather than occurring uniformly, matched what Tundra's own mixed-interval telemetry later confirmed independently. Tundra's encoding documentation now cites the paper directly alongside the original Gorilla-style design it otherwise follows most closely.

title: Ingestion Throughput and Hardware Footprint
text: A single Tundra storage node running on a 16-vCPU instance with NVMe-backed storage sustains 2.1 million samples per second of write throughput before write-ahead-log fsync latency becomes the bottleneck. The write-ahead buffer defaults to 512 megabytes per node and typically holds between 40 and 90 seconds of unsealed writes under peak load. Background compaction runs on a dedicated thread pool sized to one-quarter of available vCPUs, defaulting to 4 threads on a 16-vCPU node. A fully utilized cluster of 210 nodes, matching the largest reported deployment, sustains the previously cited 340 billion samples per day, roughly 3.9 million samples per second in aggregate. Memory footprint per active series averages 180 bytes, dominated by the in-memory block buffer rather than any per-series metadata structure.

title: How Tundra's Storage Format Evolved
text:
Tundra did not begin as a columnar store at all. The project started in 2016 inside a monitoring team frustrated with a row-oriented time-series database that stored each sample as a full row containing a series identifier, a raw 64-bit timestamp, and a raw 64-bit float, 24 bytes per sample before any indexing overhead, and no compression whatsoever. Disk costs for a single mid-sized deployment were already becoming the team's largest infrastructure line item by early 2016, which is what actually triggered the rewrite rather than any specific performance complaint.

The first working prototype, built over roughly five months, split timestamps and values into separate column files but did not compress either one; it earned its keep purely through better disk I/O locality during range scans, since a query touching only values no longer had to read timestamp bytes it did not need. Version 1.0, released in March 2017 after an eight-month private beta, added a first compression pass: simple delta encoding for both timestamps and values, cutting disk usage by roughly 60% against the uncompressed columnar layout.

The move to XOR-based value compression came in version 2.0, in November 2018, after an engineer prototyped it against a corpus of counter metrics and found it consistently beat plain delta encoding for gauge-type series whose values fluctuate rather than climb steadily. That same release paired XOR value compression with delta-of-delta timestamp compression for the first time, rather than plain deltas, since scrape-interval regularity made delta-of-delta a near-free additional win once the block-sealing infrastructure already existed.

For nearly three years the timestamp encoder used a four-bucket variable-length scheme adapted directly from published Gorilla-style designs. That changed only in 2025, when telemetry from mixed-interval deployments, clusters running 1-second, 5-second, and 60-second scrape jobs against the same block, showed the four-bucket fallback rate climbing well above what single-interval deployments experienced. The five-bucket scheme described in Tundra's current encoding documentation shipped in version 5.0, in late 2025, specifically to address that fallback-rate regression.

Tiered downsampling, by contrast, was not a compression optimization at all but a retention-cost response: version 3.0, in February 2021, introduced it after several large deployments asked for cheaper long-term retention than keeping raw data indefinitely could offer. The rewritten compaction engine that shipped alongside it remains largely intact in version 5.4 today, though the rollup tiers themselves have been retuned twice since. Looking back, the team describes the row-to-columnar switch as the one decision that made every later compression improvement possible, since none of the per-column encoders would have been nearly as effective applied across mixed-type rows.

title: Erratum: Documentation Build 5.4.2
text: Documentation build 5.4.2, published alongside the version 5.4 release notes, corrects a mislabeled axis on the block-window comparison diagram in the query-latency guide; the vertical axis had read "compression ratio" when it actually plots bytes per sample, the inverse relationship the diagram's caption already described correctly. The diagram itself was not regenerated, only the axis label, since the underlying benchmark numbers were unaffected. While auditing that section, the docs team also re-flagged the introductory paragraph, which still reads that Tundra's first production release, version 1.0, became generally available in March 2019, following an eight-month private beta that began in July 2016. Both fixes ship in this build; no further errors were found. The next documentation review accompanies the version 5.5 release.

title: Commissioning a New Storage Node
text:
Bringing a new Tundra storage node into a production cluster follows a fixed commissioning sequence before it accepts live traffic.

1. Provision NVMe-backed local storage sized for at least 45 days of raw-tier retention at the cluster's ingestion rate.
2. Set block window, compaction thread count, and write-ahead buffer size to match existing nodes rather than installer defaults.
3. Join the cluster's gossip ring and confirm a consistent block-index view within 60 seconds.
4. Run the built-in encoder self-test, sealing and decoding a synthetic 2-hour block to confirm byte-identical round trips.
5. Route a small share of read traffic to the node and compare results against an existing node for 24 hours.
6. Only after that shadow period completes without discrepancies does the node receive its full write share.

Skipping the shadow-read period is the most common cause of commissioning incidents, since encoder bugs passing the self-test can still surface only under real, irregular traffic.

title: Turkish Research on the Latency Tradeoff
text: Tundra's block-window defaults were not chosen from internal benchmarking alone; they also draw on comparative work published outside the English-language storage-systems literature. İstanbul Teknik Üniversitesi'nden Yılmaz ve Demir'in 2020 tarihli çalışması, "Büyük Ölçekli Zaman Serisi Veritabanlarında Sıkıştırma Oranı ve Sorgu Gecikmesi Dengesi," blok boyutunun sıkıştırma oranı ile sorgu gecikmesi arasındaki ödünleşimi nasıl etkilediğini gösterdi, modeling exactly the tradeoff Tundra's own block-window benchmark later measured directly. The study's finding that point-lookup latency degrades non-linearly past a certain block size, rather than growing smoothly, matched the sharp latency increase Tundra observed beyond roughly 12-hour windows. Tundra's query-latency guide references the study directly when explaining why the default window stops well short of where compression gains taper off but latency costs accelerate.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2024 Compaction Backlog
text:
On March 14th, 2024, a compaction backlog on Tundra's reference cluster caused sealed blocks to accumulate unrolled-up for nearly 11 hours, delaying rollup-tier availability for roughly 1.2 billion raw samples and triggering stale-data alerts across several downstream dashboards. The proximate cause was a configuration change deployed the previous day that reduced the compaction thread pool from 4 threads to 2 threads per node, intended to free CPU headroom for an unrelated ingestion-throughput experiment running on the same cluster. The reduced thread pool was sufficient during normal ingestion volume but fell behind once a scheduled batch of 40,000 new series began scraping simultaneously as part of an onboarding migration, roughly doubling the cluster's sustained sample rate for several hours.

Compaction queue depth grew steadily rather than spiking, which is why automated alerting, tuned to detect sudden spikes rather than slow, sustained growth, did not fire until queue depth had already exceeded four times its normal baseline, at which point an on-call engineer was paged manually by a downstream team noticing stale rollup data rather than by Tundra's own monitoring. Diagnosis took approximately 40 minutes, most of it spent ruling out a suspected encoder bug before the recently reduced thread-pool configuration was identified as the actual cause. Restoring the thread pool to 4 threads per node allowed the backlog to drain within roughly 90 minutes, and rollup-tier data was fully caught up by 22:40 UTC that evening.

No raw data was lost during the incident; the backlog affected only how promptly rollup tiers reflected recently sealed blocks, and raw-tier queries against the affected window returned correct results throughout. The postmortem's primary remediation was adding a queue-depth-based alert that fires on sustained growth over a rolling 30-minute window, rather than relying solely on threshold-crossing spike detection, which had proven too slow for this failure mode. A secondary remediation required any change to compaction thread-pool sizing to go through the same review process as changes affecting ingestion capacity directly, since the two had previously been treated as independent tuning knobs despite sharing the same underlying CPU budget. The team also added compaction queue depth as a required field in the pre-deployment capacity-review checklist for any configuration change touching CPU allocation. A follow-up review three months later confirmed no recurrence of sustained backlog growth under comparable onboarding-migration load, and the new alert has since fired correctly during two smaller, unrelated compaction slowdowns. The incident remains the reference case cited whenever a future change proposes altering compaction thread-pool sizing on a cluster where CPU budget is already shared with ingestion.

title: Footnote: Comparing Rollup Retention to CoolFrost's Approach
text: A comparison our field engineering team gets asked about often is how Tundra's rollup retention compares to CoolFrost, a competing time-series platform several prospective customers migrate from. CoolFrost's public documentation describes a single configurable rollup tier rather than Tundra's four fixed tiers, leaving retention-versus-resolution tradeoffs entirely to the operator instead of shipping defaults. That flexibility appeals to teams with unusual retention requirements but has reportedly caused misconfiguration incidents at two migrating customers who assumed CoolFrost's defaults matched Tundra's, most notably around how the one-minute rollup tier retains aggregated data for 60 days before the block is superseded by the one-hour rollup tier. Prospective migrators should treat every tier boundary as something to reconfigure explicitly rather than something to carry over unchanged.

title: Current Deployment Footprint, Q2 2026
text: As of the version 5.4 release in Q2 2026, Tundra's telemetry beacon reports 4,300 opted-in production clusters, up from 3,100 a year earlier. Combined stored data volume across reporting clusters reached 61 trillion data points, an increase of roughly 28% year over year. Average cluster size grew from 34 nodes to 41 nodes over the same period, while the largest reported single cluster held steady at 210 nodes. Version 5.x adoption reached 71% of reporting clusters within five months of the version 5.0 release, faster than version 4.0's adoption curve, which took eleven months to cross the same threshold. Support tickets tagged as compaction-related fell by 34% following the March 2024 postmortem's remediations, the largest single-category improvement recorded since telemetry reporting began in 2021.
